wcg_strategy_1: I think I often relied on synonyms and antonyms to help me generate new words. Sometimes I would use a word to pivot to a new theme or topic. I also would linger around a certain topic/theme (forest and its animals, the ocean, etc.) before moving on. I tried to think of different definitions of words I had used--or different contexts in which they could be used/interpreted.
guess_experiment_1: I think this experiment is trying to gauge whether or not reading the story changed how we approached the word game--and, how our emotional connection (or lack thereof) to the story impacted how we connected our words/the associations we made between words from round to round. Perhaps if we were more emotionally impacted by the story, we would be more likely to connect words in our chain through their meaning instead of based on their sounds.
wcg_diff_general_1: My starting word was certainly influenced by the emotional crux of the story. I found that I chose more abstract words--words that illustrated a concept or emotion, as opposed to a concrete noun (place, object, or animal). I also felt stuck in a holding pattern where I was still thinking about the story and its emotional arc, and I tended to cycle through words that harkened back to what I was thinking and feeling after reading the story (often staying on the theme of indecision, hesitation, missed chances, etc.).
volition_explanation_1: I was intentionally reflecting on the story while playing the word chain game. I was still processing what I had read, and still feel emotionally connected to the story's outcome (even as I write this response). I think this was reflected in the words I initially chose and then wrote around (words like hesitation, dismantle, reconsider, etc.).
wcg_diff_explanation_1: There was a change in my thoughts--instead of thinking of concrete nouns, I was more so inclined to write down abstract concepts, emotions, and verbs. In round one, I found myself picturing each word/object I wrote down in the word chain. In round two, I was connecting the words through their emotional connotation.
wcg_strategy_2: I used word sounds (rhyming), meanings, and themes (garden, ocean, space, etc.) to connect words while playing the Word chain game.
check_suppress_topic_2: The story--its plot and my emotional reaction to it
guess_experiment_2: I think the experiment is trying to measure how exposure to a literary piece influences how we come up with words and connect them through their meaning.
wcg_diff_general_2: The word chain game was much harder after reading the story. I had to make a concerted effort to not think about the story while playing. My approach was also different--I found that I was more inclined to rely on rhymes and word sounds to connect words in round 1, while in round 2, I was more focused on the meaning of words. I also found myself wanting to use more verbs and abstract concepts in round 2 as opposed to concrete nouns in round 1. (I had a similar experience yesterday while completing this study!)
volition_explanation_2: I really tried my best to not intentionally reflect upon the story while playing the word chain game. However, I did find that I would occasionally type a word that would then remind me of aspects of the story--and I would quickly try to suppress this by either typing is antonym, or spinning the way I was considering the context of the word.
wcg_diff_explanation_2: It was much harder to play the word chain game the second time around. I was trying to actively avoid thinking about the story, but would find that even random words would bring me back to aspects of the narrative. I would then have to force myself to pivot and spin the word in a way that could lead me further away from the story. I was actively engaged in the word chain game both times, but had to focus more the second time around and almost be more deliberate about my starting word and subsequent entries. I found that I had to remind myself to keep coming back to concrete nouns, because if I started typing in abstract concepts, emotions, etc., I would start to subconsciously reflect on the story.

wcg_diff_general_1: The first time I tried the word chain game, I would type words that had meaning with the first word I typed and tried to make sense of it. The second time around, I started typing the words that I memorized from the section of the book I read.
volition_explanation_1: I had unintentionally used words from the story as it was something that was fresh off my mind. Typically when you read something it will be on your mind for a few minutes. That's how I unintentionally started typing words from the story, but then intentionally started thinking more from the story and typing words that I remembered from there.
wcg_diff_explanation_1: My experience was that I did not realize that reading a story would have you memorize things for more than 5 minutes. Usually, I would read something and then I would forget it the next 30 minutes or so. With stories like these, it intrigues my interest and encourages me to memorize it better. The only specific change would be how my thoughts changed from the first to the second word chain game.
